common_name: Dwarf Wax Myrtle (Compact Native Evergreen Shrub for Gulf Coast Landscapes)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Wax Myrtle is exceptionally adaptable — it tolerates poor soil, sand, clay, salt spray, wet conditions, drought, and partial shade, making it one of the most versatile low-maintenance shrubs for Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Wax Myrtle ('Pumila') is a compact cultivar of the native southern wax myrtle (Morella cerifera) that offers the same extraordinary adaptability and wildlife value of the species in a smaller, more landscape-manageable scale of 3–6 feet — suitable for foundation plantings, hedges, and mixed shrub borders where the full-sized species reaching 10–15 feet would be out of scale. The aromatic, small, narrow, gray-green evergreen foliage has a pleasantly spicy, bay-like scent when crushed and provides fine-textured, dense screening year-round. Female plants (this species has separate male and female individuals) produce the characteristic small, waxy, gray-blue berries in clusters along the stems — these berries are consumed by over 40 species of birds including yellow-rumped warblers, tree swallows, bluebirds, and Carolina wrens that depend heavily on the high-fat berries during winter migration and cold weather. The deep-rooted, nitrogen-fixing root system improves lean soils over time. The standard species (full-sized wax myrtle) is one of the most important multi-habitat native shrubs of the Gulf South; 'Pumila' provides the same ecological value in residential-scale plantings. Tolerates salt spray, poor drainage, drought, and coastal conditions that challenge most ornamental shrubs.

common_name: Cuban Mint (Mojito Mint, Large-Leaf Heat-Tolerant Gulf South Spearmint)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Cuban Mint spreads by underground runners like other mints — plant in containers or with a physical root barrier to prevent spreading beyond intended areas.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Cuban Mint (also called Mojito Mint or Apple Mint hybrid) is Mentha x villosa — a hybrid mint between spearmint (M. spicata) and apple mint (M. suaveolens) that produces the distinctive large, crinkled, soft-textured, heavily aromatic leaves most closely associated with the traditional Cuban mojito cocktail. The broad, deeply textured leaves have a strong, clean spearmint fragrance with a sweeter, fruitier quality than standard spearmint — a flavor complexity that is particularly well-suited to the classic mojito (fresh Cuban Mint + lime + rum + sugar + soda) and makes excellent fresh herb tea. Cuban Mint's practical advantage over standard spearmint and peppermint for Gulf South gardeners is substantially better heat and humidity tolerance — where peppermint and even spearmint often melt out in the intense summer heat of zone 9, Cuban Mint produces reliably through Gulf South summers with consistent moisture and partial shade protection from afternoon sun. The large, broad leaves are easy to pick and produce abundantly. Spreads by vigorous underground runners in the mint family manner — grow in containers or with buried root barriers to keep contained. In zone 9, Cuban Mint may be semi-evergreen through mild winters or die back and return vigorously in spring. An indispensable herb for Gulf South gardeners who want fresh mint for mojitos, tea, and culinary use during the summer months.

common_name: Dwarf Firebush (Compact Native Hummingbird Shrub for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Firebush flowers on new growth — cut back hard (to 6–12 inches) in late winter each year to promote vigorous new growth and the most prolific flowering.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Firebush ('Compacta') is a compact cultivar of the native Hamelia patens (firebush), a Florida and Gulf Coast native shrub that is one of the most outstanding hummingbird and butterfly plants for subtropical gardens — producing dense clusters of tubular, orange-red to coral-red flowers continuously from late spring through fall on a more compact, manageable 3–4 foot plant compared to the 6–10 foot of the full-sized species. The combination of orange-red tubular flowers (ideal for hummingbirds), black-purple berries (eaten by songbirds), reddish new growth, and reliable heat/drought performance makes Dwarf Firebush one of the finest all-around wildlife shrubs for zone 9 landscapes. Ruby-throated hummingbirds visit constantly during migration (March–May and July–October) and resident Gulf South hummingbirds use it throughout the warm season. Gulf fritillary, zebra longwing, and numerous other butterfly species nectar on the flowers regularly. In zone 9, Dwarf Firebush may be killed to the ground by hard freezes but returns vigorously from the roots in spring — treat as a perennial subshrub and cut back to 6 inches in late February to promote fresh growth. In protected locations and zone 9b, the plant may remain evergreen through mild winters. The compact habit makes it proportionate for residential gardens where the full species would be too large.

common_name: Purple Spiderwort (Garden Spiderwort Hybrid with Blue-Purple Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; garden spiderworts tolerate considerable shade — they perform well in positions receiving only 3–4 hours of sun, making them useful in the partial shade category that many bright-colored perennials cannot fill.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Tradescantia x andersoniana (garden spiderwort) is a hybrid group derived from crosses among several native North American Tradescantia species, producing garden perennials with larger, showier, longer-season blooms than any individual parent species. The distinctive three-petaled, blue-violet to purple flowers (individual blooms last only one day but are produced in long succession over many weeks) open primarily in the cooler parts of the day — morning and evening — making garden spiderwort particularly charming at dawn and dusk. Named cultivars include blue-purple ('Zwanenburg Blue', 'Blue Stone'), rose-pink ('Osprey', 'Karminglut'), and white selections, providing a range of color within the hybrid group. In zone 9, garden spiderwort blooms most heavily in spring (March–May) and again in fall (September–October), going semi-dormant in the heat of summer — a pattern characteristic of many cool-season perennials in the Gulf South. The partial-shade tolerance (performing well with only 3–4 hours direct sun) makes garden spiderwort a particularly valuable perennial for the between-full-sun-and-deep-shade positions that are difficult to fill with colorful flowering plants. The strappy, blue-green foliage provides attractive texture even when not in bloom. Divide established clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor.

common_name: Flame Grass (Fall-Coloring Red-Orange Miscanthus Ornamental Grass)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; Flame Grass is earlier-blooming than most Miscanthus cultivars — it flowers in August in the Gulf South, providing fall interest beginning in late summer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; Flame Grass is the most dramatically fall-coloring Miscanthus cultivar — a relatively compact (3–5 foot) selection of maiden grass that transforms in fall from the typical blue-green summer foliage to brilliant orange-red, then fiery orange and red, mimicking the fall color display of deciduous trees in climates where such displays are uncommon. In zone 9 the fall color develops in October–November as temperatures cool, creating a warm-season grass display just as most other perennial flowers are winding down. Flame Grass is earlier-blooming than most miscanthus cultivars (August–September), producing the characteristic feathery white-silver flower plumes above the fall-coloring foliage. At the warm edge of its range (zone 9), Flame Grass performs best with some afternoon shade protection during peak summer heat and reliable moisture — the intensity of fall color may be slightly less than in cooler climates where the full color transformation is triggered by sharper temperature swings. The compact size (3–5 feet vs. 6–8 feet for standard maiden grass selections) makes Flame Grass proportionate for typical residential borders. Cut to 6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. Outstanding in naturalistic plantings where the fall color transition provides a dramatic seasonal focal point.
